payload.text: I'm a dance instructor who wants to create an app to act as a portal for my students. I would like a section for 4 classes, each with a section for patterns we worked on in that class and a section for notes. On that same page I would like a section for a video of the month. I would like another page for Media. On the media page, I would like a dropdown for a list of tutorial videos from my youtube playable in the app, and 3 sections for a list of 5 New Songs, 5 Blues Songs and 5 Practice Songs with Song Title, Artist Name and Youtube Link. I would like for all the data to be pulled from a google spreadsheet.

**Formulating Initial Concepts**

I'm starting to visualize the structure for the dance instructor's web portal. I'm focusing on core functionalities and essential pages that will provide maximum value to both the instructor and students, creating the foundation for a useful application.


**Developing Class Displays**

I'm now integrating the "Home/Classes" section, concentrating on displaying key classes and a "Video of the Month." I'm prioritizing the integration of tutorial videos via in-app dropdowns and music lists. Thinking about data sources, I'm considering using a Gemini API solution to interact with the Google Spreadsheet. I'm choosing between fetching a public CSV or using it to simulate the data.


**Considering Data Sources**

I've refined the data strategy. Given the prompt, I'm now leaning towards a `SheetService` that defaults to hardcoded data for demonstration. However, it'll seamlessly incorporate logic to fetch from a published CSV URL if the user provides one, satisfying the "frontend only" requirement. To ensure initial content, I'm also considering using Gemini to generate dance content, as requested by the instructions.


**Designing Data Fetching**

I'm solidifying the data strategy. I'll construct a `GoogleSheetsService` to fetch data from a published CSV URL. To handle missing or failed URLs, a fallback to sample data will ensure immediate UI rendering. I'll use `papaparse` for CSV parsing, adhering to the "use existing libraries" instruction. I am now rethinking Gemini: it can be used for an optional "AI Assistant" feature to provide dance tips and pattern explanations, which adds value.


**Refining Gemini Integration**

I'm now zeroing in on Gemini integration. To meet the expertise requirement, I'll leverage Gemini to create a fallback data generator. If the spreadsheet is empty, Gemini will produce generic class schedules and content to ensure the UI is populated. This approach fulfills both the user's initial prompt and showcases my Gemini API proficiency.


**Planning Key Features**

Now, I'm solidifying the core architecture and features. I'm focusing on a React/TS/Tailwind project with a simple tab bar for navigation (Classes, Media, Settings). The data layer will utilize a context/hook to fetch from a Google Sheet as a CSV. The "Classes" view will show class cards and a "Video of the Month". "Media" will have a YouTube embed with dropdown lists. Most importantly, I'll integrate Gemini as an "AI Assistant" in the Notes section to explain dance patterns or expand notes. I'm also planning to use `papaparse` for CSV parsing, and considering an iframe for YouTube.


**Detailing Gemini Implementation**

I'm now concretizing the Gemini integration. Since the instructions heavily imply the need for Gemini, I will add an "AI Dance Assistant" chat widget or an "Explain Pattern" button. This will enable Gemini to elaborate on dance moves, like explaining "The Whip" in West Coast Swing, which adds a unique layer to the functionality.

I'm also planning the file structure, specifically including `metadata.json`, `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, `types.ts`, `services/sheetService.ts`, and `services/geminiService.ts` to manage the project effectively.


**Adding Visual Elements**

I'm now focusing on the components. I'm adding `ClassCard.tsx`, which will display class information and integrate Gemini, along with `MediaSection.tsx`, `VideoPlayer.tsx`, and `Navigation.tsx`. I'm designing the Google Sheet logic with a `DEFAULT_DATA` constant for initial usability. My spreadsheet structure will use three tabs for Classes, Videos, and Music. Finally, I will implement a "Gemini Pattern explainer" next to the "Patterns" list.


**Developing Initial UI Elements**

I'm currently laying out the basic application structure. I'm focusing on the `App.tsx` file for managing tabs, the classes tab with a grid and video showcase, and a media tab including dropdown tutorials and song listings. I'm also planning the theme, opting for a studio look with white/gray, dark text, and accent colors.


**Structuring Data Types**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the TS types. I'm focusing on `ClassData`, `VideoData`, and `SongData` to handle class details, video links, and music information. To streamline data input, I'll add a "Settings" modal where the user can input the Google Sheet ID or CSV links. To facilitate the demo, I'll incorporate a "Demo Mode" flag to use local data when no sheet is provided, resolving the prompt's request for spreadsheet-based data.

I am now confident that this will correct the issue of the 4th card showing music data.


**Enforcing Class Card Limit**

I've streamlined the logic to enforce a strict class card limit. The `musicSectionStart` pre-scan is gone, and I am now removing the `i >= musicSectionStart` check. I'm focusing on ensuring the class loop in `dataService.ts` only iterates from index 1 to 4, specifically for rows 2 to 5. The loop will only add a class when the name field isn't empty. I've just verified that the row indices align with the class mapping in `dataService.ts`. The loop from index 1 to 4 correctly corresponds to rows 2 to 5. I checked `types.ts` and `ClassesView.tsx` to ensure data integrity and proper slicing. It seems the slice is correct, so it's only a matter of making sure the service is returning the expected data. I'm now zeroing in on the problematic music data display. My logic has been streamlined. I can now remove `musicSectionStart` and the "break" statement, while keeping only a loop from index 1 to 4. **Revising Buggy Detection**

I'm currently working to simplify the `dataService.ts` code. The complex section detection and URL checks are clearly unnecessary for the class card bug. I'm focusing on a more straightforward approach to ensure the correct data is always displayed, especially when there are fewer than four classes. I need a more reliable way to isolate data for class cards.


**Isolating Row Data**

I'm now zeroing in on the sheet layout constraints, specifically rows 2-5 for classes. I realize the initial "smart" music section start is unneeded, since classes are rigidly in those rows.  I have updated my plan to remove unnecessary logic and stick to the row indices 1-4. The focus is to precisely extract class data and ignore anything past the 5th row, regardless of placeholder text. The news section is still fine.


**Implementing Strict Indices**

I've realized the problem might be how `Papa.parse` handles empty rows. To adhere to the no-pre-scan rule, I'll strictly use indices 1-4. Within the loop, I'll add a check to verify that we haven't inadvertently picked up music data if the CSV export is collapsing empty rows. This will make the process much more resilient.


**Defining Music Exclusion**

I've refined the logic to handle the shifted row problem. Since I'm sticking to strict indices (1-4), I need a way to differentiate between class data and the "Music" section, which the user confirmed can shift up. My new approach involves checking the first cell of each row (`rows[i][0]`) within the loop. If it contains "Music" or "Songs", I'll safely exclude it. This should ensure only valid class data gets added, even if empty rows cause shifts. This will fix the card bug!


**Validating Music Exclusion**

I've got the `dataService.ts` code under control, with a clear plan to prevent Music data from contaminating the class data. I'm keeping the index 1-4 loop and am adding a check to identify Music headers. If the text in the first cell of a row, using `toLowerCase()`, matches "music" or "song", I'll be sure to exclude it. This will address the row shifting in the class cards, which, according to the user, should always be in the rows 2-5, or indices 1-4.


**Analyzing CSV Row Handling**

I'm now revisiting the CSV export behavior. The user's input suggests the CSV *might* be collapsing empty rows, hence the music appearing where class data should be. I'm focusing on how `Papa.parse` handles `skipEmptyLines: false` and the structure of exported CSVs. I'll verify if `rows[4]` contains music due to row collapsing during export. I'll ensure the code correctly skips empty rows and music sections by examining the content of the data. I'm adding a thorough filter for music section to ensure that the class card bug gets fixed.


**Refining Music Filter**

I'm now revising my music exclusion logic. Previously, I was checking for "music" or "songs" in the row's first cell to avoid contamination. Now, I see the user's code already has a good check: `name && name.toLowerCase() !== 'class name' && name.toLowerCase() !== 'name'`. This is a crucial filter that I can leverage to catch shifted music rows! I'll update the `dataService.ts` code accordingly, ensuring `skipEmptyLines` truly works. I'm confident this will fix the bug.


**Prioritizing URL Checks**

I'm now zeroing in on a robust solution for shifted data. The key is strict indexing from 1-4 and a check for music-related entries. I'll examine the "Notes" column for URLs, a clear indicator of song data vs. class data, which the user prefers. If `rows[4]` contains music, the notes will be a URL. I want to ensure my index check works as expected.


**Prioritizing Notes Analysis**

I'm now integrating another layer of verification to the code. Since Music starts at row 8, data from index 4 might be songs. I'll focus on the data at index 4 and examine the contents of column C. A URL there indicates a song; text notes point to class information. This approach guarantees precise data extraction for each class.

To address the issue of music data appearing in your class cards when you have fewer than four classes, I have simplified the logic in `dataService.ts`. 

The application now strictly iterates through indices 1 to 4 (representing Rows 2 to 5 of your spreadsheet). To prevent music data from "sliding up" into these indices if the spreadsheet export collapses empty rows, I've added a check to ensure the data at these indices doesn't contain music keywords or URLs in the notes column (which are characteristic of the music section). This ensures that if a class row is truly empty, the card will hide itself rather than displaying the next available piece of data.

The "News" section logic has been left exactly as it was.
